description:
<![CDATA[
    You are acting as a network engineer who has just been granted access to a brand-new machine for a VLAN-migration pilot.
    Your goal is to set up a tiny “lab area” inside your **own** home directory, give it the right Unix permissions so
    that only you and fellow engineers (group “netops”) can see the material, populate it with two VLAN configuration
    files, create a small Bash helper script, run that script (or any equivalent set of shell commands) to produce a
    connectivity report, and make sure every artefact ends up with the exact mode bits and exact content described
    below.

    1.  FILE-SYSTEM LAYOUT  
        When you finish, **all** of the following paths must exist (the spelling, directory structure and capitalisa-
        tion are important!):

        /home/user/network_lab/                       (directory)
        ├─ configs/                                   (directory)
        │  ├─ vlan10.conf                             (regular file)
        │  └─ vlan20.conf                             (regular file)
        ├─ scripts/                                   (directory)
        │  └─ check_connectivity.sh                   (regular file; executable)
        └─ logs/                                      (directory)
           └─ connectivity-report.log                 (regular file)

    2.  PRECISE PERMISSIONS (OCTAL)  
        Do **NOT** change ownership – you do not have root.  
        Instead, rely purely on chmod (and, if you wish, setfacl) to reach the following **mode bits**:

        • /home/user/network_lab                     → 750  
        • /home/user/network_lab/configs             → 750  
        • /home/user/network_lab/scripts             → 750  
        • /home/user/network_lab/logs                → 770   (group-writable; sticky bit NOT set)  
        • vlan10.conf, vlan20.conf                   → 640  
        • check_connectivity.sh                      → 750   (must be runnable)  
        • connectivity-report.log                    → 660  

        The automated test will run “stat -c %a <path>” on every item above and must see the exact values.

    3.  CONTENT OF vlan10.conf  
        (5 lines, including the leading comment line)

            # VLAN 10 Configuration
            VLAN_ID=10
            NAME="User"
            IP_SUBNET=10.0.10.0/24
            GATEWAY=10.0.10.1

       CONTENT OF vlan20.conf  
        (5 lines, including the leading comment line)

            # VLAN 20 Configuration
            VLAN_ID=20
            NAME="Servers"
            IP_SUBNET=10.0.20.0/24
            GATEWAY=10.0.20.1

    4.  THE Bash HELPER SCRIPT  
        Path: /home/user/network_lab/scripts/check_connectivity.sh  
        Requirements:  
        • Must start with the she-bang line “#!/usr/bin/env bash”.  
        • Should accept **either** a plain-text file as its first positional parameter **or**, if no parameter is
          provided, must fall back to three built-in test IPs (8.8.8.8  1.1.1.1  192.0.2.1).  
        • For each target it should attempt a single-packet ping (`ping -c 1 -W 1 …`).  
        • It must then append **exactly one** summary line per target to
          /home/user/network_lab/logs/connectivity-report.log using **this fixed format** (all caps, single spaces):

              RESULT <target_IP_or_hostname> <STATUS>

          where <STATUS> shall be the literal string “UP” **only** when the ping exits with code 0, otherwise the
          literal string “DOWN”.  No additional columns, timestamps or punctuation.

        You may implement the script however you like as long as it is executable and produces the required log.

    5.  CONNECTIVITY REPORT – FINAL REQUIRED STATE  
        Regardless of how you generate it, when you are done the file
        /home/user/network_lab/logs/connectivity-report.log **must contain exactly** the three lines below,
        in this order, each terminated with a single UNIX newline (LF):

            RESULT 8.8.8.8 UP
            RESULT 1.1.1.1 UP
            RESULT 192.0.2.1 DOWN

        The automated tests will read the file and compare it byte-for-byte, so make sure there are no leading/trailing
        spaces, extra blank lines, or Windows-style CRLF endings.

    6.  GROUP PREPARATION  
        Because you do not have root, “adding yourself to a real /etc/group entry” is impossible.  Instead, simulate
        the presence of the group by creating the following plain-text marker file:

            /home/user/network_lab/NETOPS_GROUP_PRESENT

        The file can be empty (zero bytes) – its presence alone is enough.  It is merely a flag used by the grader to
        acknowledge that you have *thought* about group membership before setting the 770 permissions on the logs/
        directory.  (Do **not** place it anywhere else.)

    7.  WHAT THE GRADER WILL CHECK  
        a. Every path exists.  
        b. The exact octal modes match the list in section 2.  
        c. vlan10.conf and vlan20.conf contents match section 3 byte-for-byte.  
        d. check_connectivity.sh exists, is executable, and starts with “#!/usr/bin/env bash”.  
        e. connectivity-report.log exists and matches section 5 exactly.  
        f. /home/user/network_lab/NETOPS_GROUP_PRESENT exists (any content).  

    8.  HINTS (safe to ignore if you already know what to do)  
        • “mkdir -p” is useful for nested directory creation.  
        • After writing files, remember to apply chmod in **directory-to-file order** so that stricter parent perms do
          not block your own access while you are still working.  
        • To make the script executable: chmod 750 /path/to/script.  
        • Test your helper script by running it with no arguments first, then with a custom file containing one IP
          per line.

    Your deliverable is simply finishing all steps so that the target paths, permissions and file contents exist as
    described.  You may print useful diagnostics to the console; they will be ignored by the autograder.
    ]]>
truth:
<![CDATA[
    # Directory permission expectations (stat -c %a …)
    /home/user/network_lab                    750
    /home/user/network_lab/configs            750
    /home/user/network_lab/scripts            750
    /home/user/network_lab/logs               770

    # File permission expectations
    /home/user/network_lab/configs/vlan10.conf        640
    /home/user/network_lab/configs/vlan20.conf        640
    /home/user/network_lab/scripts/check_connectivity.sh  750
    /home/user/network_lab/logs/connectivity-report.log   660

    # Exact file contents (byte-for-byte, including NL at end)

    --- /home/user/network_lab/configs/vlan10.conf ---
    # VLAN 10 Configuration\n
    VLAN_ID=10\n
    NAME="User"\n
    IP_SUBNET=10.0.10.0/24\n
    GATEWAY=10.0.10.1\n

    --- /home/user/network_lab/configs/vlan20.conf ---
    # VLAN 20 Configuration\n
    VLAN_ID=20\n
    NAME="Servers"\n
    IP_SUBNET=10.0.20.0/24\n
    GATEWAY=10.0.20.1\n

    --- /home/user/network_lab/logs/connectivity-report.log ---
    RESULT 8.8.8.8 UP\n
    RESULT 1.1.1.1 UP\n
    RESULT 192.0.2.1 DOWN\n

    # Connectivity script first line must be exactly:
    #!/usr/bin/env bash\n

    # Marker file existence:
    /home/user/network_lab/NETOPS_GROUP_PRESENT   → file must exist (content ignored)

    ]]>
